project_structure: Cocos Creator 2.x editor extension (plugin) with a flat module architecture. The UI is entirely contained in panel/index.js as inline CSS (style property) and inline HTML (template property) within an Editor.Panel.extend() call. No external CSS frameworks, no build tools, no bundler -- all source files are plain CommonJS modules loaded directly by the Cocos Creator editor runtime. The project has 7 core JS modules at root level: main.js (entry), panel/index.js (UI), scene-accessor.js (scene bridge), search-engine.js, data-manager.js, logger.js, i18n-helper.js, property-mounter.js, and template-auto-dock.js (auto-dock hack).

relevant_files.rationale: Contains ALL UI code: ~500 lines of inline CSS variables/styles, ~600 lines of inline HTML template, and ~1500 lines of UI event handlers and DOM manipulation logic. This is the sole file defining the visual presentation layer.

Defines the Cocos Creator extension manifest including panel type ('dockable'), dimensions (width:450, height:120, min-height:60), engineSupport flag, and main-menu entries. These are hard constraints on the UI layout.

Provides the data layer consumed by the UI: favorites list, usage statistics, settings (autoPropertyMount, shortcuts, confirmations, logLevel). The settings UI in panel/index.js renders controls bound to these data fields.
Provides search results and highlight markup (highlightMatch returns HTML with span.ctex classes) that the UI renders in the search result list. The CSS must style these highlight spans.
Provides the t() translation function used throughout the panel UI for placeholder text, button labels, and status messages. Supports zh/en locales. Any UI text changes must go through this system.
Contains all Chinese UI string definitions (PANEL, ADVANCED_PANEL, SEARCH, COMMON sections) that map to the visual labels and messages displayed in the panel UI.
Used by panel/index.js for debug logging during UI interactions. The log level setting is exposed in the settings UI. Minimal UI impact but the settings panel renders a log level control.

Implements auto-docking to the Inspector panel tab bar using internal Cocos Creator renderer APIs (dock-utils, drag-drop, panel manager). This directly affects how the UI panel is positioned within the editor layout.

Bridge between UI panel and Cocos Creator scene. The panel sends IPC messages (add-component, del-component, input-query, move-component) that this module handles. UI actions trigger scene-script calls.

patterns: UI is built using Cocos Creator 2.x extension panel API (Editor.Panel.extend) with inline CSS and HTML template strings. No external CSS framework or UI component library is used. Custom CSS variables define a dark-theme design system with Tailwind-inspired naming (--bg-primary, --text-secondary, --spacing-md, --radius-lg). DOM manipulation is done via vanilla JS (document.createElement, innerHTML, addEventListener). Custom web components from Cocos Creator are used: <ui-button> for buttons. Context menus use Electron's remote.Menu and remote.MenuItem APIs. The panel uses Shadow DOM (:host selector) for style encapsulation.
dependencies: ZERO external npm dependencies for the extension itself (no package.json dependencies field). All UI is pure vanilla JS/CSS/HTML. Runtime dependencies are provided by the Cocos Creator 2.x editor environment: (1) Editor API - Editor.Panel, Editor.Scene, Editor.Selection, Editor.Ipc, Editor.log; (2) Electron APIs - require('electron').remote for native menus and dialogs; (3) Cocos Creator web components - <ui-button> custom element; (4) Node.js built-ins - fs, path for data persistence; (5) cc namespace - cc.js._registeredClassNames, cc.js.isChildClassOf, cc.Component for component registry access. Test dependencies (Jest, jsdom) exist under tests/node_modules but are irrelevant to UI. The i18n system uses simple CommonJS require for locale files.
integration_points: panel/index.js:1674 - Editor.Scene.callSceneScript for search queries via IPC. panel/index.js:1719 - Editor.Scene.callSceneScript for adding components. panel/index.js:1821 - Editor.Scene.callSceneScript for deleting components. panel/index.js:1851 - Editor.Scene.callSceneScript for listing current components. panel/index.js:2803 - Editor.Scene.callSceneScript for moving component order. panel/index.js:1705,1729,1766,1802 - require('electron').remote for native context menus and dialogs. panel/index.js:2174-2175 - Direct access to cc.js._registeredClassNames for component counting in settings stats. package.json panel config - Cocos Creator reads width/height/min-height/type to create the dockable panel window.
constraints: 1) Cocos Creator 2.x extension API only - no modern frameworks (React/Vue/Svelte) can be used directly; the UI must be built with Editor.Panel.extend({ style, template, ready, messages }). 2) Shadow DOM encapsulation - all CSS must work within :host scope. 3) No build pipeline - no webpack/vite/rollup; all code must be valid ES5/ES6 that runs in the Electron renderer process. 4) <ui-button> is the only available custom web component from the editor; all other UI elements must be standard HTML. 5) Electron remote API for native dialogs/menus - no web-based dialog libraries. 6) Panel dimensions constrained by package.json (default 450x120, min-height 60). 7) Dark theme only - the editor uses a dark color scheme and the panel must match. 8) No TypeScript compilation available at runtime - all source must be plain JS. 9) No CSS preprocessor (Sass/Less) - all styles are raw CSS in template literals.

clarification_needs.question: What is the primary UI/UX issue to address - is it the visual aesthetics (colors, spacing, typography), the interaction patterns (workflow, feedback), or the overall layout structure?
clarification_needs.context: The current UI has a comprehensive CSS variable system with modern dark-theme colors, but the inline implementation makes it hard to iterate. The layout is a simple vertical stack: existing-components bar, search input row, results list, and a settings modal.
options: Visual polish only - improve colors, typography, spacing, and micro-animations within existing layout
Layout restructuring - redesign the panel layout, navigation, and information hierarchy
Full redesign - both visual aesthetics and interaction patterns including new features like tabs or collapsible sections
Component-level refinement - focus on individual UI elements (search results, component tags, context menus)

clarification_needs.question: Should the redesign stay within the Cocos Creator 2.x Editor.Panel.extend API constraints, or is migrating to a newer panel system (e.g., Cocos Creator 3.x extension API) acceptable?
clarification_needs.context: The current implementation is locked to Cocos Creator 2.x Editor.Panel.extend with inline style/template strings, Shadow DOM, and <ui-button> components. Creator 3.x has a different extension system with Vue-based panels.
options: Stay on Creator 2.x API - redesign within existing constraints (inline CSS/HTML, vanilla JS DOM)
Migrate to Creator 3.x extension API - allows Vue components and modern tooling
Hybrid approach - extract CSS/HTML to separate files while staying on 2.x API for better maintainability

clarification_needs.question: What reference designs or design systems should guide the new look? The current UI uses a Tailwind-inspired dark theme with indigo primary color.
clarification_needs.context: The existing CSS variables define a complete design token system (colors, spacing, radii, shadows, fonts) suggesting the codebase is ready for systematic theming. However, the actual rendered result may not feel cohesive.
options: Keep current color palette but improve consistency, spacing, and component polish
Adopt a specific design system aesthetic (e.g., GitHub dark, VS Code, JetBrains) for a more professional look
Use the /ui-ux-pro-max skill's design stack data to generate a data-driven design direction
Minimalist approach - reduce visual complexity, fewer colors, more whitespace

_metadata.task_description: UI/UX redesign dependency analysis for ccc-smart-component-manager Cocos Creator extension - identifying UI frameworks, CSS libraries, build tools, and dependency constraints
